sections.heading: Key-Value Storage in Android
sections.content: Android provides lightweight key-value storage for saving small pieces of data such as user preferences, login sessions, app settings, and UI state flags. Two main solutions exist: the older SharedPreferences API and the modern Jetpack DataStore library. Both store data as key-value pairs, but DataStore is built with Kotlin coroutines and Flow, making it asynchronous, safer, and better suited for modern Android architecture.

sections.heading: Common Use Cases
items: User preferences like dark mode or notification settings
Session information such as auth tokens or user IDs
App state flags like onboarding completion
UI state like last selected tab or search query
Avoid using key-value storage for large or relational data — use Room database instead

rows.datastore: Asynchronous API using coroutines and Flow

rows.shared_preferences: May block main thread and cause ANR
Legacy API used in older apps
rows.datastore: Recommended by Google for new apps

sections.heading: SharedPreferences Overview
sections.content: SharedPreferences stores data in an XML file inside the app's internal storage. Developers read and write values using simple get and put methods. While easy to use, it performs synchronous disk operations which can block the main thread. Because of this limitation and lack of proper error handling, it is considered a legacy solution.

sections.heading: Jetpack DataStore
sections.content: Jetpack DataStore is the modern replacement for SharedPreferences. It uses Kotlin coroutines and Flow to provide asynchronous, safe, and consistent data access. DataStore emits updates whenever preferences change, allowing UI layers to react automatically. There are two variants: Preferences DataStore for simple key-value data and Proto DataStore for strongly typed schema-based storage.

sections.heading: DataStore Key Features
items: Built on Kotlin Flow for reactive updates
Better error handling compared to SharedPreferences
Works well with MVVM and repository architecture

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: SharedPreferences was the original Android key-value storage solution, but its synchronous API and thread safety issues make it less suitable for modern apps. Jetpack DataStore provides a safer, coroutine-based alternative that integrates naturally with Flow and MVVM architecture. For new Android applications, DataStore is the recommended way to persist simple user preferences and app settings.
